quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Logistic DE"?

options.text: Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
∫csc²x dx = -cot x + C; ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C; ∫csc x cot x dx = -csc x + C
dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K); growth rate is proportional to both population size AND the remaining fraction of capacity
Given f(a) and f(b) with continuity/differentiability: MVT guarantees f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for some c between a and b

quiz_questions.explanation: "Logistic DE" is defined as: dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K); growth rate is proportional to both population size AND the remaining fraction of capacity. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Carrying capacity (K)"?

options.text: f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)
The maximum sustainable population; as P → K, dP/dt → 0 and growth stops
d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out of the derivative
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.

quiz_questions.explanation: "Carrying capacity (K)" is defined as: The maximum sustainable population; as P → K, dP/dt → 0 and growth stops. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Logistic solution"?

options.text: Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function)
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀; produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve
Use similar triangles to relate the heights and distances of an object and its shadow; differentiate to find shadow length rate

quiz_questions.explanation: "Logistic solution" is defined as: P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀; produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Inflection point at P = K/2"?

options.text: f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
The population is growing fastest when P = K/2; the growth rate dP/dt is maximum
Average value has the same units as f(x); the (1/(b-a)) factor divides out the length units
P = K: if the population is perturbed slightly above or below K, it returns to K

quiz_questions.explanation: "Inflection point at P = K/2" is defined as: The population is growing fastest when P = K/2; the growth rate dP/dt is maximum. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Equilibrium solutions"?

options.text: F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1
d/dx[f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x); derivative of a sum/difference is the sum/difference of derivatives
P = 0 (unstable: any small population grows) and P = K (stable: populations near K return to K)
Given f(a) and f(b) with continuity/differentiability: MVT guarantees f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for some c between a and b

quiz_questions.explanation: "Equilibrium solutions" is defined as: P = 0 (unstable: any small population grows) and P = K (stable: populations near K return to K). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K); growth rate is proportional to both population size AND the remaining fraction of capacity"?

options.text: Logistic DE
Differential equation (DE)
Speed vs. velocity
Tangent lines to implicit curves

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Logistic DE".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The maximum sustainable population; as P → K, dP/dt → 0 and growth stops"?

options.text: Carrying capacity (K)
Conservative forces
Lorenz curve
Infinite limit

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Carrying capacity (K)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀; produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve"?

options.text: When to simplify first
Logistic solution
MVT with restrictions
Limit laws

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Logistic solution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The population is growing fastest when P = K/2; the growth rate dP/dt is maximum"?

options.text: Inflection point at P = K/2
Extreme Value Theorem
Tangent lines to implicit curves
Arctan integral

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Inflection point at P = K/2".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P = 0 (unstable: any small population grows) and P = K (stable: populations near K return to K)"?

options.text: Equilibrium solutions
Combined technique
Definite integral
Integration by parts formula

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Equilibrium solutions".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Logistic Growth is accurate?

options.text: Logistic Growth models population growth with limited resources: dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K), where K is the carrying capacity.
The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees there exists a c in [a,b] where f(c) = f_avg—the function actually achieves its average value at some point.

The method: (1) identify the quantity to optimize, (2) express it as a function of one variable using constraints, (3) find critical points where f'(x) = 0, (4) verify maxima/minima using the first or second derivative test, (5) check endpoints...
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Logistic Growth as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Logistic Growth?

options.text: Success requires clear justification of setup, correct antiderivatives, appropriate use of technology, and precise interpretation of results in the given context.
Unlike exponential growth, logistic growth slows as P approaches K and levels off.
It is the theoretical foundation for many calculus results.
Basic Derivative Rules provide shortcuts for finding derivatives without using the limit definition every time.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Logistic Growth as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Logistic Growth?

options.text: As n→∞, Riemann sums converge to the definite integral: ∫ₐᵇf(x)dx = lim(n→∞) Σf(xᵢ*)Δx.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x).
The solution is P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀.
Implicit Differentiation finds dy/dx when y is not explicitly solved as a function of x.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Logistic Growth as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Logistic Growth?

options.text: These are important for integration and solving applied problems.
The inflection point occurs at P = K/2, where the population is growing fastest.
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.
Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Logistic Growth as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Logistic Growth?

options.text: Trigonometric substitutions handle expressions with √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²).

Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).
These derivatives are essential for modeling growth, decay, and natural phenomena.
The growth curve is S-shaped (sigmoidal), starting exponentially and flattening near K.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Logistic Growth as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Logistic Growth?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years
Species population modeling: deer populations in managed forests follow logistic growth, with K determined by food and habitat availability
Climate data: identifying periods of warming (increasing temperature trend) vs. cooling using derivative analysis of temperature time series
Financial analysis: if an investment grows from $1000 to $1200 over a year, MVT guarantees the instantaneous growth rate was exactly 20% at some moment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Logistic Growth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Logistic Growth?

options.text: Numerical integration in computer science: trapezoidal and Simpson's rules are programmed into calculators and software for approximating integrals when antiderivatives don't exist in closed form
Temperature modeling: since temperature changes continuously, the IVT guarantees that between any two measured temperatures, every intermediate value was achieved
Disease spread: the SIR model's infection curve resembles logistic growth, with the inflection point marking peak new infections
Electrical engineering: integrating sinusoidal voltage V(t) = V₀sin(ωt) across a capacitor to find charge: Q = ∫V dt = -(V₀/ω)cos(ωt) + C

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Logistic Growth.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Logistic Growth applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse
Finding the slope of a circle x² + y² = 25 at any point: dy/dx = -x/y, giving the tangent line direction for navigation and engineering applications
Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Market saturation: adoption of new technology (smartphones, streaming services) follows an S-curve as the product approaches market saturation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Logistic Growth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Logistic Growth?

options.text: Total rainfall accumulation: if r(t) is the rainfall rate at time t, then total accumulated rainfall by time x is R(x) = ∫₀ˣ r(t)dt
Engineering: computing cross-sectional area of unusually shaped beams or channels for structural and flow analysis
Species population modeling: deer populations in managed forests follow logistic growth, with K determined by food and habitat availability
Probability: the Cauchy distribution has CDF (1/π)arctan(x) + 1/2, obtained by integrating the probability density 1/(π(1+x²))

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Logistic Growth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Logistic Growth in practice?

options.text: Quality control: setting an acceptable error margin (ε) for a product property and determining the input precision (δ) needed in the manufacturing process
Population dynamics: given a birth rate minus death rate function, calculate total population change over a decade
Disease spread: the SIR model's infection curve resembles logistic growth, with the inflection point marking peak new infections
Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Logistic Growth.
